skill.name: typescript-strict
skill.description: Enforce TypeScript strict mode and type safety. Use when setting up projects, reviewing code, or when type errors are ignored. Covers strict flags, no-any rules, and type inference best practices.
skill.summary: Enforce strict TypeScript settings, safer types, and code review checks.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: The static external command alerts are primarily false positives from Markdown backticks and TypeScript code examples. The skill does allow Bash, Edit, and Write, but the documented commands are local TypeScript checks and initialization commands with no destructive behavior. The hardcoded URLs are reference links, and no prompt injection or exfiltration intent was found.

low_findings.title: Broad Local Tool Permissions
low_findings.description: SKILL.md grants Bash, Edit, and Write permissions. This is appropriate for TypeScript configuration work, but it can modify project files and run local commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The permissions are explicitly declared in the skill metadata. No malicious command or destructive file operation is present.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Local Examples
low_findings.description: The confirmed command examples run TypeScript initialization, strict checks, and grep against local project files. The other static command alerts are Markdown backticks around TypeScript examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is documentation and code examples. The bash block uses fixed commands such as npx tsc and grep, with no user-controlled shell interpolation.

low_findings.title: Documentation Reference URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to TypeScript, typescript-eslint, and Zod documentation. They are reference links, not network calls or data exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in the References section. No code sends data to these domains.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The static blocker at line 3 matches descriptive TypeScript text, not a cryptographic algorithm. No hashing, encryption, or credential handling code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is only the skill description. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alert Is False Positive
low_findings.description: The static system reconnaissance alert maps to a TypeScript error handling example. It does not inspect host state, users, processes, or network configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 82 throws an Invalid data error inside a sample type guard. There is no command or API call that performs reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: TypeScript projects often lose safety when strict checks, any usage, and ignored errors spread. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code enforce strict configuration and safer typing patterns.
actual_capabilities: Recommend strict tsconfig settings for TypeScript projects.
Identify unsafe any usage and suggest unknown or generic alternatives.
Replace risky type assertions with type guards where practical.
Guide null safety with strictNullChecks and optional chaining.
Suggest typescript-eslint rules for stricter type safety.
Provide review checklists for strict TypeScript code.
limitations: It provides guidance and edits, but it is not a full static analyzer.
It may need project-specific context before changing shared type definitions.
It does not guarantee that runtime validation is complete.
Some migrations require incremental fixes across many files.

use_cases.title: Set up a strict TypeScript project
use_cases.description: Configure tsconfig and lint rules before unsafe patterns become common.

use_cases.title: Review pull requests for type safety
use_cases.description: Check for any, ts-ignore, unsafe assertions, and missing null handling.

use_cases.title: Migrate legacy TypeScript code
use_cases.description: Plan a staged migration from loose typing to strict compiler settings.

prompt_templates.title: Check my strict settings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my TypeScript configuration and tell me which strict options are missing or disabled.

prompt_templates.title: Remove unsafe any usage
prompt_templates.prompt: Find any usage in this file and replace it with unknown, generics, or specific interfaces where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Review type assertions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this TypeScript change for unsafe type assertions, non-null assertions, and missing runtime validation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan strict migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged migration plan to enable strict TypeScript checks across this project with minimal disruption.

output_examples.input: Review a tsconfig file for strict TypeScript readiness.
output: Flags missing: strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, and exactOptionalPropertyTypes.
Recommended next step: enable strict first, then fix null and implicit any errors.

output_examples.input: Replace unsafe any in a data parsing helper.
output: Add a type guard before reading properties.
Use a schema validator when the data crosses a trust boundary.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that uses type assertions.
output: Risk found: direct assertion from response data to a User type.
Suggested fix: validate shape before use and narrow the type through a guard.

best_practices: Enable strict compiler options before adding new application code.
Use unknown at trust boundaries until data is validated.
Prefer type guards and schema validation over broad type assertions.
anti_patterns: Do not use any to silence errors during migration.
Do not add ts-ignore without a documented temporary reason.
Do not cast external response data directly to application types.

faq.question: Does this skill change project files?
faq.answer: It can guide edits and may update configuration or TypeScript files when requested.

faq.question: Can it enable strict mode in an existing project?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help plan and apply strict mode incrementally.

faq.question: Does it ban all type assertions?
faq.answer: No. It recommends minimizing assertions and using guards when the value is uncertain.

faq.question: Can it work with ESLint?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends typescript-eslint rules that support strict type safety.

faq.question: Does it validate runtime data?
faq.answer: It can suggest validation patterns, but application-specific runtime validation still needs review.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
